title: Polycythaemia Vera
oneLiner: Chronic myeloproliferative neoplasm with excess red cell mass → high thrombotic risk; requires cytoreduction and phlebotomy to prevent stroke/MI/PE.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute arterial/venous thrombosis** (stroke, MI, PE, DVT) – **single finding:** sudden focal neurology, pleuritic chest pain with hypoxia, unilateral painful swollen leg.
- **Haemorrhage** (especially GI from acquired von Willebrand disease when platelets >1000) – **single finding:** melaena or haematemesis with shock despite high haemoglobin.
- **Hyperviscosity syndrome** (when Hct >0.60) – **single finding:** confusion, blurred vision, or transient ischaemic attacks without focal deficit.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Headache, dizziness, tinnitus
  - Pruritus (classically after hot bath – histamine-mediated)
  - Blurred vision / transient visual loss (amaurosis fugax)
  - Erythromelalgia (burning pain in hands/feet with erythema)
  - Hypertension (increased blood volume)
  - Gout symptoms (hyperuricaemia)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Plethora (ruddy cyanosis of face, palms, conjunctivae)
  - Splenomegaly (common – up to 70% of patients)
  - Hepatomegaly (less common)
  - Fundoscopy: engorged retinal veins ("cherry-red" appearance)
  - Hypertension (systolic/diastolic elevation)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Secondary polycythaemia (most likely mimic)** – high EPO, normal O2 sats, absent JAK2 mutation, history of smoking/lung disease/sleep apnoea/renal tumour.
- **Essential Thrombocythaemia** – isolated thrombocytosis, Hct normal, JAK2 positive in ~50%, less splenomegaly.
- **Chronic myeloid leukaemia** – leucocytosis (typically >50), left shift, splenomegaly, Philadelphia chromosome positive.
- **Myelofibrosis** – anaemia, teardrop cells, massive splenomegaly, dry tap on bone marrow.
- **Relative polycythaemia (stress polycythaemia)** – normal red cell mass, low plasma volume (e.g. dehydration, smoking), JAK2 negative.
- **Splitter:** JAK2 V617F mutation present in >95% of PV patients – if positive, PV is confirmed and secondary causes effectively excluded; if negative, consider secondary causes and test for JAK2 exon 12.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - O2 saturations (if <94% → suggests secondary hypoxic polycythaemia)
  - BP (hypertension common)
  - ECG if chest pain / palpitations
  - Blood glucose (headache/dizziness also hypoglycaemia mimic)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC with differential: Hb, Hct, WBC, platelet count
  - Ferritin, iron, TIBC (iron deficiency can occur due to increased erythropoiesis and phlebotomies)
  - Uric acid (often high – gout risk)
  - LDH (elevated in myeloproliferation)
  - Coagulation screen (prolonged PT/APTT if acquired von Willebrand)
  - JAK2 V617F mutation (gold standard test; high sensitivity)
  - Serum erythropoietin (EPO) – low in PV, normal/high in secondary polycythaemia
  - Arterial blood gas: check pO2 and CO2 if sleep apnoea suspected
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR (to exclude lung pathology causing hypoxia)
  - Abdominal US (assess splenomegaly, exclude renal tumour)
- **Special tests:**
  - Bone marrow aspiration and trephine biopsy (if diagnosis uncertain or transformation suspected) – shows trilineage myeloproliferation with increased megakaryocytes
- **Diagnostic criteria:** WHO 2016 criteria – major criteria:
    1. Hb >165 g/L (men) or >160 g/L (women) or Hct >0.49/0.48
    2. JAK2 V617F or exon 12 mutation
    3. If JAK2 negative: low serum EPO + bone marrow biopsy showing trilineage myeloproliferation with increased megakaryocytes
  - Diagnosis requires either both major criteria, or first major + minor (low EPO + bone marrow findings).
- **Never-miss test:** JAK2 V617F mutation test – omission leads to missed diagnosis and inappropriate management. Also check O2 saturations – missing secondary polycythaemia causes inappropriate cytoreduction.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute thrombosis: urgent CT head (stroke), CTPA (PE), duplex leg (DVT) → anticoagulation per local protocol after excluding haemorrhage.
  - If suspected hyperviscosity (Hct >0.60 with neurological symptoms): urgent phlebotomy 250–500 mL (monitor BP, replace volume with colloid if hypotensive). [Check local policy for phlebotomy in acute setting.]
  - If acute haemorrhage (GI): IV fluids, cross-match, gastroenterology review; avoid aspirin/anticoagulants; treat acquired von Willebrand disease with desmopressin or vWF concentrate if severe.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Initiate regular phlebotomy to target Hct <0.45 (NICE) – usually 250–500 mL every 2–7 days depending on starting Hct and symptoms.
  - Start low-dose aspirin 75 mg PO once daily unless contraindicated (platelet >1000, active bleeding, acquired vWD).
  - Assess thrombotic risk (age >60, prior thrombosis, thrombocytosis, cardiovascular risk factors) to decide need for cytoreductive therapy.
  - High-risk patients (age >60, prior thrombosis, platelets >1500): start hydroxycarbamide (hydroxyurea) [check local protocol for dose: typically 500–2000 mg PO daily, titrated to maintain Hct <0.45 and platelets <400].
  - Consider allopurinol 100–300 mg PO once daily if uric acid elevated or history of gout – monitor for rash (allopurinol hypersensitivity).
  - Correct iron deficiency cautiously (iron replacement can exacerbate erythrocytosis; only give if severe symptomatic iron deficiency or after phlebotomy).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Maintenance phlebotomy every 1–3 months as needed.
  - Continue aspirin lifelong (unless contraindicated).
  - Cytoreductive therapy: first-line hydroxycarbamide; second-line interferon-alfa or ruxolitinib (if intolerant or hydroxycarbamide fails).
  - Monitor for progression: annual FBC, symptoms of splenomegaly, constitutional symptoms (weight loss, night sweats – may indicate transformation to myelofibrosis).
  - Manage pruritus: antihistamines (e.g. cetirizine 10 mg OD) or cimetidine 400 mg BD (H2 antagonist).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride IV at 125 mL/hour if Hct >0.55 (to reduce viscosity) – but avoid overhydration if heart failure risk.
  - VTE prophylaxis: consider LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) **only after** checking platelet count – if platelets >1000, acquire von Willebrand screen first; **do not start** LMWH without senior haematology advice if platelets >1000 (bleeding risk). For moderate thrombocytosis (platelets 500–1000), LMWH is usually safe. [Check local policy.]
  - Diet/NBM: as per clinical status; no specific restriction unless for procedure.
  - Observations: baseline NEWS2; if phlebotomised, monitor post-procedure for hypotension.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Aspirin: 75 mg PO once daily (lifelong) – reduces thrombotic risk.
  - Hydroxycarbamide (hydroxyurea): 500 mg – 2 g PO once daily. Start at 500 mg daily, titrate upwards every 2–4 weeks targeting Hct <0.45 and platelets <400. Monitor FBC weekly during titration. [Check local policy for exact threshold doses.]
  - Phlebotomy: 250–500 mL per session; frequency guided by Hct (e.g. every 2–7 days initially).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Allopurinol: 100–300 mg PO once daily (for gout/hyperuricaemia).
  - Anagrelide: 0.5–2 mg PO daily (for essential thrombocythaemia; rarely used in PV).
  - Ruxolitinib: 5–10 mg PO twice daily (second-line for hydroxycarbamide-intolerant or resistant patients with splenomegaly).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Aspirin: avoid if active bleeding, platelet >1000, acquired von Willebrand disease (coagulation screen prolonged).
  - Hydroxycarbamide: teratogenic – avoid in pregnancy; myelosuppressive – dose reduce if WBC <3.0 or platelets <100.
  - Ruxolitinib: risk of infection (herpes zoster, TB), dose reduce in renal impairment (CrCl <30 mL/min), avoid in severe hepatic impairment.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Hydroxycarbamide: caution in renal impairment (eGFR <30) – [check local policy]; may need dose reduction.
  - Anagrelide: no significant adjustment.
  - Ruxolitinib: start 5 mg BD (reduce from 10 mg) if CrCl 30–59; avoid if <30.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Hydroxycarbamide** – starting dose too high (e.g. 2g/day) can cause severe myelosuppression (pancytopenia). Always titrate from 500 mg daily and check FBC weekly until stable.
  - **Aspirin** – giving aspirin to a patient with platelet >1000 before checking for acquired von Willebrand disease can cause catastrophic GI bleed. Always check PT/APTT and von Willebrand screen before starting aspirin if platelets >1000.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Thrombotic events: stroke, TIA, MI, DVT, PE, deep vein thrombosis of unusual sites (portal, hepatic, cerebral venous sinuses)
  - Haemorrhage: GI, epistaxis, post-surgical bleeding
  - Hyperviscosity syndrome: headache, visual disturbance, confusion, seizure
  - Gout: acute monoarthritis
- **Chronic:**
  - Myelofibrosis (post-PV myelofibrosis) – anaemia, massive splenomegaly, weight loss, night sweats
  - Acute myeloid leukaemia (AML) – risk ~5–10% at 10 years, higher with alkylating agents
  - Splenomegaly (causing early satiety, splenic infarcts, pain)
  - Systemic hypertension
  - Iron deficiency (from repeated phlebotomies)
sections.Prognosis:
- Median survival ~15–20 years with optimal cytoreduction and phlebotomy.
- Thrombotic risk falls to near-normal if Hct maintained <0.45 and aspirin used.
- Transformation to AML or myelofibrosis occurs in ~10–20% of patients over 20 years – poor prognosis (shorter survival).
- High-risk patients (age >60, prior thrombosis, high platelet count) have greatest benefit from cytoreduction.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Confirm diagnosis with JAK2 mutation** – do not start cytoreduction without it unless urgent.
- **Start aspirin ASAP** (after ruling out bleeding risk) – reduces cardiovascular events by 50%.
- **Use phlebotomy aggressively** to achieve Hct <0.45 – that single step is the most powerful thromboprophylaxis.
- **Treat pruritus** with antihistamines (e.g. cetirizine) or cimetidine – it is a hallmark symptom and affects quality of life.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give iron supplements routinely** – they will stimulate erythropoiesis and worsen Hct, and reduce the effect of phlebotomy. Only give if symptomatic iron deficiency with ferritin <15.
- **Do not diagnose PV without checking O2 sats** – up to 30% of high Hct cases are secondary (COPD, sleep apnoea, renal tumour). Missing secondary cause leads to unnecessary cytoreduction.
- **Do not stop antiplatelet therapy post-thrombosis** unless bleeding occurs – continuing aspirin is critical for secondary prevention.
- **Do not use heparin high-dose in thrombotic event without checking platelet count** – if platelets >1000, the patient may have acquired von Willebrand disease; start low-molecular-weight heparin only after haematology advice.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Sudden weakness, speech difficulty, facial droop (stroke symptoms)
  - New chest pain, breathlessness, or leg swelling/fever (thrombosis/PE)
  - Black stools, vomiting blood, heavy bruising (bleeding)
  - Severe headache with visual changes (hyperviscosity)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Haematology outpatient clinic: within 2 weeks of discharge for first review, then 1–3 monthly for phlebotomy and dose titration.
  - GP for blood pressure monitoring and cardiovascular risk management.
  - Advise to stop smoking, maintain healthy weight (reduces secondary polycythaemia factors).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (haematology registrar/consultant) if:**
  - Hct >0.60 despite phlebotomy (immediate crisis)
  - Platelet count >1500 (immediate bleeding risk)
  - Suspected stroke/TIA, PE, DVT (after initial stabilisation)
  - Acute haemorrhage requiring transfusion and reversal of von Willebrand defect
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or any single parameter at 3, e.g. RR >30, GCS <13)
- **Call ICU if:**
  - Haemodynamic instability from haemorrhage or thrombosis requiring vasopressors
  - Seizure or coma (hyperviscosity crisis)
  - Acute leukaemia transformation with sepsis (neutropenic fever)
- **Crash team (2222) if:**
  - Cardiac

rawMarkdown:
## Polycythaemia Vera
**One-liner:** Chronic myeloproliferative neoplasm with excess red cell mass → high thrombotic risk; requires cytoreduction and phlebotomy to prevent stroke/MI/PE.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute arterial/venous thrombosis** (stroke, MI, PE, DVT) – **single finding:** sudden focal neurology, pleuritic chest pain with hypoxia, unilateral painful swollen leg.
- **Haemorrhage** (especially GI from acquired von Willebrand disease when platelets >1000) – **single finding:** melaena or haematemesis with shock despite high haemoglobin.
- **Hyperviscosity syndrome** (when Hct >0.60) – **single finding:** confusion, blurred vision, or transient ischaemic attacks without focal deficit.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Headache, dizziness, tinnitus
  - Pruritus (classically after hot bath – histamine-mediated)
  - Blurred vision / transient visual loss (amaurosis fugax)
  - Erythromelalgia (burning pain in hands/feet with erythema)
  - Hypertension (increased blood volume)
  - Gout symptoms (hyperuricaemia)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Plethora (ruddy cyanosis of face, palms, conjunctivae)
  - Splenomegaly (common – up to 70% of patients)
  - Hepatomegaly (less common)
  - Fundoscopy: engorged retinal veins ("cherry-red" appearance)
  - Hypertension (systolic/diastolic elevation)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Secondary polycythaemia (most likely mimic)** – high EPO, normal O2 sats, absent JAK2 mutation, history of smoking/lung disease/sleep apnoea/renal tumour.
- **Essential Thrombocythaemia** – isolated thrombocytosis, Hct normal, JAK2 positive in ~50%, less splenomegaly.
- **Chronic myeloid leukaemia** – leucocytosis (typically >50), left shift, splenomegaly, Philadelphia chromosome positive.
- **Myelofibrosis** – anaemia, teardrop cells, massive splenomegaly, dry tap on bone marrow.
- **Relative polycythaemia (stress polycythaemia)** – normal red cell mass, low plasma volume (e.g. dehydration, smoking), JAK2 negative.
- **Splitter:** JAK2 V617F mutation present in >95% of PV patients – if positive, PV is confirmed and secondary causes effectively excluded; if negative, consider secondary causes and test for JAK2 exon 12.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - O2 saturations (if <94% → suggests secondary hypoxic polycythaemia)
  - BP (hypertension common)
  - ECG if chest pain / palpitations
  - Blood glucose (headache/dizziness also hypoglycaemia mimic)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC with differential: Hb, Hct, WBC, platelet count
  - Ferritin, iron, TIBC (iron deficiency can occur due to increased erythropoiesis and phlebotomies)
  - Uric acid (often high – gout risk)
  - LDH (elevated in myeloproliferation)
  - Coagulation screen (prolonged PT/APTT if acquired von Willebrand)
  - JAK2 V617F mutation (gold standard test; high sensitivity)
  - Serum erythropoietin (EPO) – low in PV, normal/high in secondary polycythaemia
  - Arterial blood gas: check pO2 and CO2 if sleep apnoea suspected
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR (to exclude lung pathology causing hypoxia)
  - Abdominal US (assess splenomegaly, exclude renal tumour)
- **Special tests:**
  - Bone marrow aspiration and trephine biopsy (if diagnosis uncertain or transformation suspected) – shows trilineage myeloproliferation with increased megakaryocytes
- **Diagnostic criteria:** WHO 2016 criteria – major criteria:
    1. Hb >165 g/L (men) or >160 g/L (women) or Hct >0.49/0.48
    2. JAK2 V617F or exon 12 mutation
    3. If JAK2 negative: low serum EPO + bone marrow biopsy showing trilineage myeloproliferation with increased megakaryocytes
  - Diagnosis requires either both major criteria, or first major + minor (low EPO + bone marrow findings).
- **Never-miss test:** JAK2 V617F mutation test – omission leads to missed diagnosis and inappropriate management. Also check O2 saturations – missing secondary polycythaemia causes inappropriate cytoreduction.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute thrombosis: urgent CT head (stroke), CTPA (PE), duplex leg (DVT) → anticoagulation per local protocol after excluding haemorrhage.
  - If suspected hyperviscosity (Hct >0.60 with neurological symptoms): urgent phlebotomy 250–500 mL (monitor BP, replace volume with colloid if hypotensive). [Check local policy for phlebotomy in acute setting.]
  - If acute haemorrhage (GI): IV fluids, cross-match, gastroenterology review; avoid aspirin/anticoagulants; treat acquired von Willebrand disease with desmopressin or vWF concentrate if severe.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Initiate regular phlebotomy to target Hct <0.45 (NICE) – usually 250–500 mL every 2–7 days depending on starting Hct and symptoms.
  - Start low-dose aspirin 75 mg PO once daily unless contraindicated (platelet >1000, active bleeding, acquired vWD).
  - Assess thrombotic risk (age >60, prior thrombosis, thrombocytosis, cardiovascular risk factors) to decide need for cytoreductive therapy.
  - High-risk patients (age >60, prior thrombosis, platelets >1500): start hydroxycarbamide (hydroxyurea) [check local protocol for dose: typically 500–2000 mg PO daily, titrated to maintain Hct <0.45 and platelets <400].
  - Consider allopurinol 100–300 mg PO once daily if uric acid elevated or history of gout – monitor for rash (allopurinol hypersensitivity).
  - Correct iron deficiency cautiously (iron replacement can exacerbate erythrocytosis; only give if severe symptomatic iron deficiency or after phlebotomy).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Maintenance phlebotomy every 1–3 months as needed.
  - Continue aspirin lifelong (unless contraindicated).
  - Cytoreductive therapy: first-line hydroxycarbamide; second-line interferon-alfa or ruxolitinib (if intolerant or hydroxycarbamide fails).
  - Monitor for progression: annual FBC, symptoms of splenomegaly, constitutional symptoms (weight loss, night sweats – may indicate transformation to myelofibrosis).
  - Manage pruritus: antihistamines (e.g. cetirizine 10 mg OD) or cimetidine 400 mg BD (H2 antagonist).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride IV at 125 mL/hour if Hct >0.55 (to reduce viscosity) – but avoid overhydration if heart failure risk.
  - VTE prophylaxis: consider LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) **only after** checking platelet count – if platelets >1000, acquire von Willebrand screen first; **do not start** LMWH without senior haematology advice if platelets >1000 (bleeding risk). For moderate thrombocytosis (platelets 500–1000), LMWH is usually safe. [Check local policy.]
  - Diet/NBM: as per clinical status; no specific restriction unless for procedure.
  - Observations: baseline NEWS2; if phlebotomised, monitor post-procedure for hypotension.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Aspirin: 75 mg PO once daily (lifelong) – reduces thrombotic risk.
  - Hydroxycarbamide (hydroxyurea): 500 mg – 2 g PO once daily. Start at 500 mg daily, titrate upwards every 2–4 weeks targeting Hct <0.45 and platelets <400. Monitor FBC weekly during titration. [Check local policy for exact threshold doses.]
  - Phlebotomy: 250–500 mL per session; frequency guided by Hct (e.g. every 2–7 days initially).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Allopurinol: 100–300 mg PO once daily (for gout/hyperuricaemia).
  - Anagrelide: 0.5–2 mg PO daily (for essential thrombocythaemia; rarely used in PV).
  - Ruxolitinib: 5–10 mg PO twice daily (second-line for hydroxycarbamide-intolerant or resistant patients with splenomegaly).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Aspirin: avoid if active bleeding, platelet >1000, acquired von Willebrand disease (coagulation screen prolonged).
  - Hydroxycarbamide: teratogenic – avoid in pregnancy; myelosuppressive – dose reduce if WBC <3.0 or platelets <100.
  - Ruxolitinib: risk of infection (herpes zoster, TB), dose reduce in renal impairment (CrCl <30 mL/min), avoid in severe hepatic impairment.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Hydroxycarbamide: caution in renal impairment (eGFR <30) – [check local policy]; may need dose reduction.
  - Anagrelide: no significant adjustment.
  - Ruxolitinib: start 5 mg BD (reduce from 10 mg) if CrCl 30–59; avoid if <30.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Hydroxycarbamide** – starting dose too high (e.g. 2g/day) can cause severe myelosuppression (pancytopenia). Always titrate from 500 mg daily and check FBC weekly until stable.
  - **Aspirin** – giving aspirin to a patient with platelet >1000 before checking for acquired von Willebrand disease can cause catastrophic GI bleed. Always check PT/APTT and von Willebrand screen before starting aspirin if platelets >1000.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Thrombotic events: stroke, TIA, MI, DVT, PE, deep vein thrombosis of unusual sites (portal, hepatic, cerebral venous sinuses)
  - Haemorrhage: GI, epistaxis, post-surgical bleeding
  - Hyperviscosity syndrome: headache, visual disturbance, confusion, seizure
  - Gout: acute monoarthritis
- **Chronic:**
  - Myelofibrosis (post-PV myelofibrosis) – anaemia, massive splenomegaly, weight loss, night sweats
  - Acute myeloid leukaemia (AML) – risk ~5–10% at 10 years, higher with alkylating agents
  - Splenomegaly (causing early satiety, splenic infarcts, pain)
  - Systemic hypertension
  - Iron deficiency (from repeated phlebotomies)

### Prognosis
- Median survival ~15–20 years with optimal cytoreduction and phlebotomy.
- Thrombotic risk falls to near-normal if Hct maintained <0.45 and aspirin used.
- Transformation to AML or myelofibrosis occurs in ~10–20% of patients over 20 years – poor prognosis (shorter survival).
- High-risk patients (age >60, prior thrombosis, high platelet count) have greatest benefit from cytoreduction.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Confirm diagnosis with JAK2 mutation** – do not start cytoreduction without it unless urgent.
- **Start aspirin ASAP** (after ruling out bleeding risk) – reduces cardiovascular events by 50%.
- **Use phlebotomy aggressively** to achieve Hct <0.45 – that single step is the most powerful thromboprophylaxis.
- **Treat pruritus** with antihistamines (e.g. cetirizine) or cimetidine – it is a hallmark symptom and affects quality of life.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give iron supplements routinely** – they will stimulate erythropoiesis and worsen Hct, and reduce the effect of phlebotomy. Only give if symptomatic iron deficiency with ferritin <15.
- **Do not diagnose PV without checking O2 sats** – up to 30% of high Hct cases are secondary (COPD, sleep apnoea, renal tumour). Missing secondary cause leads to unnecessary cytoreduction.
- **Do not stop antiplatelet therapy post-thrombosis** unless bleeding occurs – continuing aspirin is critical for secondary prevention.
- **Do not use heparin high-dose in thrombotic event without checking platelet count** – if platelets >1000, the patient may have acquired von Willebrand disease; start low-molecular-weight heparin only after haematology advice.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Sudden weakness, speech difficulty, facial droop (stroke symptoms)
  - New chest pain, breathlessness, or leg swelling/fever (thrombosis/PE)
  - Black stools, vomiting blood, heavy bruising (bleeding)
  - Severe headache with visual changes (hyperviscosity)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Haematology outpatient clinic: within 2 weeks of discharge for first review, then 1–3 monthly for phlebotomy and dose titration.
  - GP for blood pressure monitoring and cardiovascular risk management.
  - Advise to stop smoking, maintain healthy weight (reduces secondary polycythaemia factors).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (haematology registrar/consultant) if:**
  - Hct >0.60 despite phlebotomy (immediate crisis)
  - Platelet count >1500 (immediate bleeding risk)
  - Suspected stroke/TIA, PE, DVT (after initial stabilisation)
  - Acute haemorrhage requiring transfusion and reversal of von Willebrand defect
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or any single parameter at 3, e.g. RR >30, GCS <13)
- **Call ICU if:**
  - Haemodynamic instability from haemorrhage or thrombosis requiring vasopressors
  - Seizure or coma (hyperviscosity crisis)
  - Acute leukaemia transformation with sepsis (neutropenic fever)
- **Crash team (2222) if:**
  - Cardiac
